stem: The surface \( z = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy \) has, at the point where \( (x, y) = ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), a normal vector of the form \( \langle f_{x},\, f_{y},\, -1 \rangle \). What is the \( y \)-component \( f_{y} \) of this normal vector?
solution: Writing the surface as \( F = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy - z \), a normal vector is \( \nabla F = \langle f_{x}, f_{y}, -1 \rangle \). Here \( f_{y} = 2([[b]])y + ([[c]])x \), which at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the \( x \)-component \( f_{x} = 2([[a]])x + ([[c]])y \), not the \( y \)-component.
This drops the factor of 2 from differentiating \( [[b]]y^{2} \).
This differentiates the cross term with respect to the wrong variable, using \( y \) instead of \( x \).
This is the function value \( f([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), not a component of the normal vector.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: The surface \( [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + [[c]]z^{2} = [[k]] \) is given implicitly by \( F(x,y,z) = [[a]]x^{2}+[[b]]y^{2}+[[c]]z^{2}-[[k]] \). At the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], [[z0]]) \), which lies on the surface, a normal vector is \( \nabla F = \langle F_{x}, F_{y}, F_{z} \rangle \). What is the \( z \)-component \( F_{z} \) of this normal vector at that point?
distractor_rationales: This is \( F_{x} \), the \( x \)-component of the normal vector, not \( F_{z} \).
This is \( F_{y} \), the \( y \)-component of the normal vector, not \( F_{z} \).
This drops the factor of 2 from differentiating \( [[c]]z^{2} \).
This returns the constant \( k \) instead of a component of the normal vector.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: The surface \( z = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[c]])xy \) has, at the point where \( (x, y) = ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), normal line parametrized as \( (x, y, z) = ([[x0]] + tf_{x},\ [[y0]] + tf_{y},\ z_{0} - t) \), where \( f_{x}, f_{y} \) are the partial derivatives at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) and \( z_{0} \) is the surface height there. What is the \( x \)-coordinate of the point on this normal line at \( t = [[t1]] \)?
solution: Here \( f_{x} = 2([[a]])x + ([[c]])y \), which at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) equals \( 2([[a]])([[x0]]) + ([[c]])([[y0]]) \). The \( x \)-coordinate on the normal line at parameter \( t=[[t1]] \) is \( [[x0]] + [[t1]]\cdot f_{x} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This moves along the normal line in the wrong direction (subtracts instead of adds \( t\,f_{x}\)).
This drops the base coordinate \( [[x0]] \) entirely.
This uses the \( z \)-coordinate's formula \( x_{0}+t \), forgetting to scale by \( f_{x} \).
This drops the factor of 2 from differentiating \( [[a]]x^{2} \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: The surface \( z = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy \) passes through the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], [[z0]]) \). The normal line there is \( (x, y, z) = ([[x0]] + t f_{x},\ [[y0]] + t f_{y},\ [[z0]] - t) \). What is the \( x \)-coordinate of the point where this normal line meets the plane \( z = 0 \)?
solution: Setting the \( z \)-coordinate \( [[z0]] - t = 0 \) gives \( t = [[z0]] \). Since \( f_{x} = 2([[a]])x+([[c]])y \) evaluated at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) is \( 2([[a]])([[x0]])+([[c]])([[y0]]) \), the \( x \)-coordinate at this \( t \) is \( [[x0]] + [[z0]]\cdot f_{x} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This moves in the wrong direction along the normal line (subtracts instead of adds).
This drops the base coordinate \( [[x0]] \) entirely.
This assumes \( f_{x}=1 \), ignoring the actual slope of the surface.
This uses \( f_{y} \) instead of \( f_{x} \) for the \( x \)-coordinate's direction.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: A surface is given parametrically by \( \mathbf{r}(u, v) = \langle u,\ v,\ [[a]]u^{2} + [[b]]v^{2} + ([[c]])uv \rangle \). A normal vector at a point is \( \mathbf{r}_{u} \times \mathbf{r}_{v} \), where \( \mathbf{r}_{u} = \langle 1, 0, \partial z/\partial u\rangle \) and \( \mathbf{r}_{v} = \langle 0, 1, \partial z/\partial v\rangle \), giving \( \mathbf{r}_{u}\times\mathbf{r}_{v} = \langle -\partial z/\partial u,\ -\partial z/\partial v,\ 1\rangle \). What is the \( x \)-component of this normal vector at \( (u, v) = ([[u0]], [[v0]]) \)?
solution: Here \( \partial z/\partial u = 2([[a]])u+([[c]])v \), so the \( x \)-component of \( \mathbf{r}_{u}\times\mathbf{r}_{v} \) is \( -\partial z/\partial u \), which at \( ([[u0]],[[v0]]) \) equals \( -(2([[a]])([[u0]])+([[c]])([[v0]])) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign that comes from the cross-product formula.
This is the (negated) \( y \)-component \( -\partial z/\partial v \), not the \( x \)-component.
This is the \( y \)-component \( \partial z/\partial v \) without the negative sign.
This drops the factor of 2 from differentiating \( [[a]]u^{2} \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: The surface \( xyz = [[k]] \) is given implicitly by \( F(x,y,z) = xyz - [[k]] \). At the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], [[z0]]) \), which lies on the surface, a normal vector is \( \nabla F = \langle F_{x}, F_{y}, F_{z} \rangle \). What is the \( x \)-component \( F_{x} \) of this normal vector at that point?
distractor_rationales: This is \( F_{y} = xz \), the \( y \)-component, not \( F_{x} \).
This is \( F_{z} = xy \), the \( z \)-component, not \( F_{x} \).
This returns the constant \( k \) instead of a component of the normal vector.
This adds \( y_{0} \) and \( z_{0} \) instead of multiplying them.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: The elevation of a hillside is given by \( z = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy \), where \( x \) and \( y \) are east-west and north-south coordinates (in km) and \( z \) is elevation (in km). A surveyor at the location \( (x, y) = ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) needs the vector perpendicular to the hillside there, \( \langle f_{x},\, f_{y},\, -1 \rangle \). What is the \( x \)-component \( f_{x} \) of this vector at the surveyor's location?
solution: Writing the surface as \( F = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy - z \), a normal vector is \( \nabla F = \langle f_{x}, f_{y}, -1 \rangle \). Here \( f_{x} = 2([[a]])x + ([[c]])y \), which at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the \( y \)-component \( f_{y} = 2([[b]])y + ([[c]])x \), not the \( x \)-component.
This drops the factor of 2 from differentiating \( [[a]]x^{2} \).
This differentiates the cross term with respect to the wrong variable.
This is the elevation \( f([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), not a component of the normal vector.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
